title: “Contemporary” Political Epochs
caption: Table III from The Decline of the West, Vol. I. The political-history table — note that the third column is now China, not Arabia. Read a row across to see the states, dynasties and statesmen Spengler holds to be 'contemporary': feudalism, the aristocratic state, absolutism, revolution, and finally money-democracy giving way to Cæsarism. The Western Civilization rows reach into the future; a few cells carry editorial notes where the printed table is anomalous (verified against Project Gutenberg #72344).
source: Decline of the West, Vol. I — Table III, “Contemporary” Political Epochs (foldout at end of volume).

rows.label: Primitive folk. Tribes and their chiefs. As yet no “politics” and no “state”.
Early Period — organic articulation of political existence; the two prime classes (noble and priest); feudal economics, purely agrarian values.
Feudalism. Spirit of countryside and countryman; the “City” only a market or stronghold; chivalric-religious ideals; struggles of vassals among themselves and against the overlord.

egyptian.text: Feudal conditions of the IV Dynasty. Increasing power of the feudatories and priesthoods. The Pharaoh as incarnation of Ra.

apollinian.text: The Homeric kingship. Rise of the nobility (Ithaca, Etruria, Sparta).

chinese.text: The central ruler (Wang) pressed hard by the feudal nobility.

faustian.text: Roman-German Imperial period. Crusading nobility. Empire and Papacy.

rows.label: Crisis and dissolution of patriarchal forms. From feudalism to the aristocratic State.

egyptian.text: VI Dynasty: break-up of the Kingdom into heritable principalities. VII and VIII Dynasties: interregnum.

apollinian.text: Aristocratic synoecism. Dissolution of kinship into annual offices. Oligarchy.

faustian.text: Territorial princes. Renaissance towns. Lancaster and York. 1254: interregnum.

rows.label: Late Period — the matured State-idea actualized; town versus countryside; rise of the Third Estate (bourgeoisie); victory of money over landed property.
Fashioning of a world of States of strict form. Frondes.

egyptian.text: XIth Dynasty: overthrow of the baronage by the rulers of Thebes. Centralized bureaucracy-state.

apollinian.text: 6th Century: First Tyrannis (Cleisthenes, Periander, Polycrates, the Tarquins). The City-State.

chinese.text: Period of the “Protectors” (Ming-Chu, 685–591) and the rulers of Thebes; congresses of princes (to 460).
chinese.note: “and the rulers of Thebes” appears in this cell in the original edition (confirmed against Project Gutenberg #72344); it also appears in the Egyptian column, and is most likely an error in the printed table, but it is transcribed here as printed.

faustian.text: Dynastic family-power; the Fronde (Richelieu, Wallenstein, Cromwell), about 1630.

rows.label: Climax of the State-form (“Absolutism”). Unity of town and “Society”; the “three estates”.

egyptian.text: XIIth Dynasty (2000–1788). Strictest centralization of power. Court and finance nobility.

apollinian.text: The pure Polis (absolutism of the Demos). Agora politics. Rise of the tribunate. Themistocles, Pericles.

chinese.text: Chun-Chiu period (“Spring and Autumn”), 590–480. Seven powers. Perfection of social forms (Li).

faustian.text: Ancien Régime. Rococo. Court nobility of Versailles. Cabinet politics. Habsburg and Bourbon. Louis XIV. Frederick the Great.

rows.label: Break-up of the State-form (Revolution and Napoleonism). Victory of the city over the countryside, of the “people” over the privileged, of money over policy.

egyptian.text: 1788–1680: revolution and military government. Decay of the realm; small potentates, in some cases sprung from the people.

apollinian.text: 4th Century: social revolution and Second Tyrannis (Dionysius I, Jason of Pherae, Appius Claudius the Censor). Alexander. Revolutions and annihilation-wars.

chinese.text: 480: beginning of the Chan-Kwo period. 441: fall of the Chou dynasty.

faustian.text: End of the 18th Century: revolution in America and France (Washington, Fox, Mirabeau, Robespierre). Napoleon.

rows.label: Domination of money (“Democracy”). Economic powers permeating the political forms and authorities.

egyptian.text: 1680 (1788)–1580: Hyksos period; deepest decline; dictatures of alien generals (Chian). 288: the imperial title. After 1600: definitive victory of the rulers of Thebes.

apollinian.text: 300–100: political Hellenism. From Alexander to Hannibal and Scipio, royal all-power; from Cleomenes III and C. Flaminius (220) to C. Marius, radical demagogues.

chinese.text: 480–230: the period of the “Contending States”. The imperialist statesmen of Tsin. From 289: incorporation of the last states in the Empire.

faustian.text: 1800–2000: the 19th Century, from Napoleon to the World-War — the “system of the Great Powers”, standing armies, constitutions; the 20th-Century transition from constitutional to informal sway of individuals; annihilation-wars; imperialism.

rows.label: Formation of Cæsarism. Victory of force-politics over money; the nations sink into a formless population, ruled as an imperium of growing crudity.

chinese.text: 250–0–26: the House of Wang-Cheng and the Western Han Dynasty. 221: the Augustus-title (Shi) of the Emperor Hwang-Ti. 140–80: Wu-ti.

faustian.note: “1000–1200” as printed in the original edition (confirmed against Project Gutenberg #72344). The value is anomalous — the West's Cæsarism is elsewhere a forward projection (cf. the preceding “1800–2000”) — but it is what the table prints.

rows.label: Maturing of the final form. Private and family policies of individual leaders; the world as spoil; Egypticism, Mandarinism, Byzantinism; ossification even of the imperial machinery.

egyptian.text: 1350–1205: XIXth Dynasty. Sethos I. Rameses II.

apollinian.text: 100–300: Trajan to Aurelian. Trajan, Septimius Severus.

faustian.note: “after 1200” as printed in the original edition (confirmed against Project Gutenberg #72344); anomalous for the same reason as the preceding row, but transcribed as printed.
